source: Learning bash with Rocky - Using Variables: Storing information: creating, quoting, and referencing variables

questions.q: How do you assign a value to a variable in bash?
options: variable=value, with no spaces around the = sign
questions.why: = assigns the content; there must be no space before or after it.

options: It must start with a letter, then letters/digits/underscore; no other special characters
It may start with a digit
questions.why: Names start with a letter; underscore is the only special character allowed.

questions.q: What naming convention does the book give?
options: Lowercase for user variables; uppercase for globals that should not be modified
questions.why: User variables are lower case by convention; upper case is used for globals/constants.

questions.q: Having done file=myfile, how do you use (expand) that variable?
questions.why: Prefix the name with a dollar sign: $file.

questions.q: Why quote a variable, e.g. touch "$file"?
options: So a value containing spaces is treated as one word, not split into several arguments
It makes the command run faster
questions.why: If $file holds a space, unquoted it creates two files; "$file" keeps it a single argument.

questions.q: How do you isolate a variable name from adjacent text, e.g. to append a 1?
questions.why: Braces delimit the name; $file1 would instead look up a variable called file1. Systematic braces are recommended.

questions.q: What do single quotes (apostrophes) do to $message inside them?
options: Inhibit interpretation -- the text stays literal ($message is not expanded)
questions.why: Single quotes inhibit special-character interpretation; double quotes allow variable expansion.
